```json
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "A global logistics platform expanding driver earnings visibility across three jurisdictions with materially different labor classifications, legal obligations, and institutional approval requirements. The feature must reconcile a single product concept ('earnings transparency') with divergent legal realities: mandatory disclosure (California), institutional gatekeeping (Germany), and voluntary disclosure (Indonesia).",
    "bounded_context": "Driver Compensation & Earnings Reporting",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Base Pay", "definition": "The guaranteed compensation a driver receives for availability or per-unit-time worked, independent of delivery volume. For employees (CA), this must meet or exceed minimum wage. For contractors (Indonesia), this is the agreed platform rate." },
      { "term": "Per-Delivery Bonus", "definition": "Variable compensation earned per completed delivery, calculated by the platform based on distance, urgency, demand multipliers, or other factors." },
      { "term": "Tip", "definition": "A voluntary gratuity from the customer to the driver. Legally and financially distinct from platform-sourced compensation. Must never be commingled with or offset against base pay or bonuses." },
      { "term": "Deduction", "definition": "Any amount subtracted from gross earnings before net payout. Includes platform fees, equipment charges, insurance contributions, tax withholdings, or penalty adjustments. The composition varies by jurisdiction and driver classification." },
      { "term": "Net Payout", "definition": "The final amount disbursed to the driver after all deductions. Must equal gross earnings minus the sum of all itemized deductions." },
      { "term": "Gross Earnings", "definition": "The sum of base pay, per-delivery bonuses, and tips before any deductions are applied." },
      { "term": "Driver Classification", "definition": "The legal labor status of the driver in their operating jurisdiction: 'employee' (CA under AB5) or 'independent contractor' (Indonesia). Determines which disclosure and wage-floor rules apply." },
      { "term": "Betriebsrat (Works Council)", "definition": "The elected employee representative body in German workplaces. Under BetrVG §87(1)(6), it holds co-determination rights over technical systems that monitor employee performance or compensation. Approval is a legal prerequisite to deploying compensation display changes in Germany." },
      { "term": "Earnings Period", "definition": "The time window over which an earnings breakdown is calculated and displayed. The spec says 'real time' but the granularity (per-delivery, per-shift, rolling window) is undefined." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "The net payout displayed must always equal gross earnings minus the sum of all itemized deductions. No hidden or unaccounted-for adjustments.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Arithmetic integrity of financial data. If this is violated, the system is displaying incorrect financial information — a data corruption issue, not a policy choice.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any earnings period: net_payout == (base_pay + per_delivery_bonus + tips) - sum(deductions). A discrepancy of even $0.01 is a defect."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Tips must never be used to offset, reduce, or satisfy base pay or minimum wage obligations. Tips are a separate earnings line item at all times.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a legal constraint in California (CA Labor Code §351) and a universal domain truth in logistics: tips belong to the driver and are not platform compensation. Violating this is wage theft, not a policy difference.",
      "testable_assertion": "Removing all tips from the earnings breakdown must not change the base pay or per-delivery bonus amounts. Minimum wage compliance calculations must exclude tip amounts entirely."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "In California, the full earnings breakdown (base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, and every individual deduction with description and amount) must be displayed to the driver. Disclosure is not optional.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "California wage transparency law and AB5 employee classification make this a legal mandate. The platform cannot choose to hide components from employee-classified drivers in CA.",
      "testable_assertion": "A California driver's earnings view must contain all four earnings components and a line-item list of deductions. Omitting any component is a compliance violation."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "In California, deductions from employee wages must each be individually authorized and itemized with a description of purpose and amount. Blanket or aggregated deduction lines are insufficient.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "CA Labor Code §224 restricts allowable deductions from employee wages to those authorized by law or the employee. Display must reflect this by itemizing each deduction so the driver can verify authorization.",
      "testable_assertion": "Each deduction displayed to a CA driver must have: a human-readable description, the legal/contractual basis, and the exact dollar amount. A single 'Total Deductions' line without itemization fails compliance."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "The compensation display feature must not be deployed to drivers in Germany until explicit written Betriebsrat approval is obtained and recorded in the system.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "BetrVG §87(1)(6) gives works councils co-determination rights over systems that display or monitor compensation. Deploying without approval is a legal violation that can result in injunctions, not merely a policy preference.",
      "testable_assertion": "The feature flag or rollout gate for Germany must check for a recorded Betriebsrat approval event. If absent, the feature must not render for any German-jurisdiction driver."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "For employee-classified drivers (California, Germany), base pay per hour worked must meet or exceed the jurisdiction's minimum wage, and this must be verifiable from the displayed breakdown.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Minimum wage is a legal floor. If the displayed breakdown shows effective hourly compensation below minimum wage, the platform is either underpaying (illegal) or miscalculating (data integrity failure).",
      "testable_assertion": "For any completed shift: base_pay / hours_worked >= jurisdiction_minimum_wage. The earnings display must surface enough data (hours, base pay) for this to be independently verifiable."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "In Indonesia, drivers are independent contractors. The platform may display earnings breakdowns voluntarily but is not legally required to itemize deductions or disclose calculation methodology.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a business policy choice, not a legal mandate. The platform could choose to show more or less detail in Indonesia based on competitive strategy or driver satisfaction goals.",
      "testable_assertion": "Indonesian driver earnings views must show at minimum: gross earnings and net payout. Additional detail (deduction itemization, calculation methodology) is optional and configurable."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "Earnings data must be segmented by jurisdiction. A driver's earnings view must reflect only the rules, classifications, and display requirements of their operating jurisdiction.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Applying the wrong jurisdiction's rules to a driver's earnings is a compliance failure. A German driver shown a CA-formatted view (or vice versa) could receive incorrect legal disclosures.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a driver operating in jurisdiction X, their earnings breakdown format, required fields, and deduction rules must match jurisdiction X's configuration. Cross-jurisdiction contamination is a defect."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A driver in California completes a delivery",
      "when": "They view their earnings breakdown",
      "then": "They see base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips (if any), and every deduction individually itemized with description and amount, and the net payout equals gross minus deductions to the cent"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "The platform has not received Betriebsrat approval for Germany",
      "when": "A German driver attempts to access the earnings transparency feature",
      "then": "The feature is not visible or accessible; the driver sees no change from current behavior"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "Betriebsrat approval has been obtained and recorded",
      "when": "A German driver views their earnings breakdown",

      "id": "AC-4",

    {
      "id": "AC-5",

    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "Any driver in any jurisdiction views their earnings",

    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "A California employee-driver works a shift",
      "when": "The earnings breakdown is displayed",
      "then": "The effective hourly base pay (excluding tips and bonuses) is visible and meets or exceeds California minimum wage"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "A driver operates across jurisdiction boundaries (e.g., starts a delivery in one jurisdiction, completes in another)", "expected_behavior": "The system must apply a deterministic jurisdiction assignment rule (e.g., pickup location, driver's home jurisdiction, or delivery destination).     { "scenario": "A deduction would reduce a California employee's effective pay below minimum wage", "expected_behavior": "The deduction must be blocked or flagged.     { "scenario": "Customer rescinds a tip after it was already displayed in the driver's earnings", "expected_behavior": "The earnings breakdown must be updated to reflect the tip reversal as a separate, visible adjustment line — not by silently modifying the original tip amount.     { "scenario": "Betriebsrat grants conditional approval (e.g., 'approved but deductions must not be shown in real time, only at end of pay period')", "expected_behavior": "The German earnings display must be configurable to respect works council conditions.     { "scenario": "Multiple deliveries complete near-simultaneously and the driver views earnings 'in real time'", "expected_behavior": "Each delivery's earnings must be atomically calculated and displayed — no partial state where one delivery's bonus is shown but its deductions are not yet applied.     { "scenario": "Platform changes deduction structure (e.g., new insurance fee) mid-pay-period", "expected_behavior": "New deductions apply only to earnings accrued after the change effective date.     { "scenario": "Driver has zero deliveries in a period but has a guaranteed minimum (e.g., CA minimum wage for on-shift time)", "expected_behavior": "The earnings breakdown must still render, showing base pay for hours worked with zero delivery bonuses and zero tips.     { "scenario": "Currency or tax regime differences for a driver who relocates between jurisdictions", "expected_behavior": "The driver's jurisdiction classification must be updated through a formal process. Historical earnings remain displayed under the rules of the jurisdiction in which they were earned. Going-forward earnings reflect the new jurisdiction." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "California non-compliance exposes the platform to class-action wage theft lawsuits (potentially millions in penalties under PAGA).
```
